# Questions in Livestream 57

Live Stream #57: This Business Is Our Fifty-Seven

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=515" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:35</a> | Does 2 Kings 19:35 conflict with Hezekiah still appearing as an Assyrian vassal, or is that a revision of the Bible? | No. He treated the event as historically complex rather than contradictory: Sennacherib failed to win cleanly, but the evidence does not let us reduce the whole episode to a simple reversal. | He framed the Assyrian crisis as one of the best documented Old Testament events but still not clear-cut. The biblical account and Assyrian evidence leave unanswered details about what happened to Sennacherib's army; Sennacherib did not return with an uncomplicated victory, yet Jerusalem also would have suffered under siege, so the episode is better read as a complex conflict with no simple winner rather than a contradiction or later revision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=807" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:27</a> | Is it true that we have actually found mummies of ancient Egyptian pharaohs? | Yes. He said many royal mummies survive, especially from Dynasties 17 through 19, with Ramesses II and Tutankhamun as famous examples. | He answered yes and named Ramesses II and Tutankhamun as famous examples. He explained that many royal mummies were recovered from protective caches such as DB320, where priests relocated and identified bodies after tomb robbery; the survival of so many royal bodies, especially from Dynasties 17-19, has even allowed modern genetic testing and kinship study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=1044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:24</a> | What can be said about the historical context of Jonah, the Assyrian Empire in that period, and its king? | He placed Jonah in the reign of Jeroboam II, when Assyria under Shalmaneser IV and Ashur-dan III was weakened, pressured, and not yet in its later imperial resurgence. | He dated Jonah by connecting Jonah son of Amittai in 2 Kings 14 to Jeroboam II, whose reign he placed around 782-753 BC. In that window Assyria was under Shalmaneser IV and Ashur-dan III, but he described the empire as weakened by opposition, plague, and internal pressure, which helps explain why Nineveh could be vulnerable before Assyria's later resurgence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=1300" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:40</a> | Does early Hebrew use of modified hieratic numerals point to Israelite origins in Egypt? | Not necessarily by itself. He said it shows Egyptian influence, but that influence could also have come through Egyptian-controlled Canaanite settings and garrison scribal practice. | He said the numeral system is real evidence of Egyptian influence, but not necessarily direct proof of Israelite origins in Egypt. Egyptian armies and administrators had operated in Canaan for a long time, and New Kingdom garrison towns used Egyptian scribes to monitor tribute and record numerical values, so Canaanite scribal practice could have absorbed Egyptian-style numerals through that setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=1487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:47</a> | Are the hailstones in Joshua 10:11 really plausible as a deadly battlefield event? | Yes. He argued that rare but severe hail could kill large numbers, especially against lightly protected Bronze Age troops. | He treated the event as plausible because extremely large hail can be lethal, and ancient troops would not have had modern shelter or protection. He read Joshua 10:11 as describing large stones from heaven and argued that severe hail falling on fleeing, exposed soldiers could account for more deaths than sword combat without requiring the event to be ordinary weather. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=1837" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:37</a> | Are there ancient sources showing that `40 years` and `1,000 years` can function idiomatically rather than as strict literals? | Yes. He used the Mesha Stele for `40` as an idiomatic number and said both biblical and patristic usage support `1,000` as a figure for a very long span. | He said ancient usage gives good reason to treat some numbers idiomatically. The Mesha Stele uses forty in a way that does not function as exact arithmetic, and he said biblical and later Christian usage can use a thousand as a large, complete, or indefinitely long span rather than a strictly counted total. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=2057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:17</a> | Can a global-flood view be steel-manned in a way that still fits the pyramids? | He said American young-earth chronology runs into major historical problems with the pyramids, though broader or less rigid flood models are easier to reconcile. | He said the main difficulty is not dinosaurs but chronology: a tightly literal young-earth flood date around 2350 BC collides with the established age and development of the pyramids. He criticized attempts to compress Egyptian chronology as inconsistent, while allowing that broader flood models or less rigid genealogical readings are easier to fit than the standard American young-earth scheme. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=2642" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:02</a> | Could he explain the ba and ka distinction in ancient Egyptian thought? | He said the ba is the personality, emotions, passions, and sense of humor; the ka is life energy or living essence; and the akh is the intellectual component. | He separated the Egyptian components of the person rather than treating them as interchangeable. The ba was the individual personality and emotional character, the ka was the life force or animating essence that needed sustaining, and the akh was the transfigured or intellectual aspect, so later interpretations should not collapse them into a single modern idea of "soul." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=2847" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:27</a> | To what extent were Egyptian pharaohs worshiped as living gods during their lifetimes? | He said this was really true only from Amenhotep III through the end of Dynasty 20; before and after that, kings were more like gods in potential until death. | He said full living-god status belongs to a limited period from Amenhotep III through the end of Dynasty 20. Before that, kings were gods in potential and became divine at death; Amenhotep III began borrowing divine temple imagery for royal furniture and space, while after Dynasty 20 Egypt's political and religious order shifted back toward the older "god in potential" pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=3092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:32</a> | What is his take on the missing verses in Jeremiah's Dead Sea Scroll material, and how do they figure in terms of inspiration? | He said he had not studied Jeremiah's authorship and textual issues enough to give a useful answer. | He declined to give a substantive judgment because Jeremiah's textual and authorship issues were not an area he had studied closely. He noted that he could not cover every subject and said the question had stumped him, so the supported answer is simply that he did not know enough to responsibly resolve the inspiration issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=3190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:10</a> | Where do the Sea Peoples' invasions fit into the conquest of Canaan? | He agreed they may have softened Canaanite targets, with Sea Peoples pressing from the coast while Israelites pressed from the east and Egypt's control weakened. | He said the Sea Peoples were an important part of the Late Bronze collapse context. Philistines and other Sea Peoples pressed into the coastal plain from the west while Israelites pressed from the east, squeezing Canaanite cities already weakened by population and political stress; Egypt's ability to control the coastal route also declined, making the region more vulnerable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=3444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:24</a> | Does the similarity between the bronze serpent and the staff of Asclepius point to Jewish influence on Greek culture and religion? | Not necessarily. He favored convergent symbolism, while allowing possible Egyptian or Jewish influence but saying the origin is hard to prove. | He said the similarity is real but not enough to prove Jewish influence. He favored convergent symbolism, noted that the Asclepius cult begins early enough to make direct Jewish influence uncertain, and allowed that Egyptian contact or diaspora Jewish contact could be possible while stressing that the actual origin is hard to determine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=3572" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:32</a> | If leprosy lives in Egypt's deserts, why not in deserts such as Arizona and Nevada, and what does it live on? | He said Hansen's disease does exist broadly, including North America, but transmission is limited; soil exposure, poverty, footwear, and other vectors affect outbreaks. | He corrected the premise by saying Hansen's disease is found globally, including North America, though outbreaks are uneven. He mentioned mysterious vectors such as armadillos, soil bacteria, and conditions associated with poverty and lack of footwear, with Brazil, India, sub-Saharan Africa, and Egypt as examples where exposure patterns make cases more visible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=3769" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:49</a> | During whose judgeship did Merneptah attack Israel, and why would Judges not mention it? | He placed it during the conquest rather than the judges period, said Joshua would be the more likely setting, and noted biblical chroniclers often omit historically important events. | He said Merneptah's campaign belongs around the conquest setting, not neatly inside a named judgeship, so Joshua would be the more likely narrative frame. He added that biblical historical books often omit events that modern historians would consider important, so silence in Judges would not by itself be surprising. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=4175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:35</a> | How could there be Nephilim before and after the flood? | He rejected Heiser's angelic-hybrid view and connected Nephilim to fallen priestly figures produced by broken celibacy vows, so a similar social-religious pattern could recur after the flood. | He rejected the idea that Nephilim require angel-human hybrids and instead connected them to a recurring priestly or social-religious failure: men who broke vows and produced powerful, socially disruptive offspring. On that reading, a similar kind of figure could appear before and after the flood without needing the same individuals or an angelic bloodline to survive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=4472" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:32</a> | How does that view of the Nephilim harmonize with them being characterized as men of renown? | He pointed to figures such as Sargon of Akkad as an analogy: offspring of broken priestly vows could be feared as powerful, disruptive, and socially consequential. | He said "men of renown" can fit people who become famous or feared because of social power, violence, and illegitimate status rather than supernatural ancestry. He used Sargon of Akkad as an analogy for a socially disruptive, important figure and argued that offspring tied to broken priestly vows could gain a reputation that later tradition remembered as renown. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=4771" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:31</a> | How can a preacher cover more Bible without simply preaching faster? | He said the aim should be deeper expository preaching, not speed: take smaller passages, explain them carefully, and balance Old and New Testament teaching over time. | He said preaching more Bible is not solved by speaking faster or rushing through large sections. His model was deeper exposition of smaller passages, careful explanation of the text, and a long-term balance that gives congregations both Old and New Testament teaching without flattening either into quick summaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=5216" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:56</a> | What books, scholars, or resources would he recommend for studying ancient Near Eastern history and religion? | He recommended resources by Kitchen, John Walton, Richard Hess, James Hoffmeier, Brent Strawn, and his own Ark of the Covenant book, among others. | He said most of the useful resources are books and named several authors rather than one single curriculum. He praised Old Testament background resources, Kitchen, John Walton, Richard Hess on Israelite religion, James Hoffmeier, Brent Strawn's work on the Old Testament, Kenny Rhodes' book, and his own Ark of the Covenant book as an Egypt-focused resource. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=5392" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:52</a> | How should Mark 10:18 be understood in a Trinitarian context when Unitarians use it as evidence? | He said Jesus is not denying his goodness or deity; he is using a rhetorical question to expose the rich young ruler's flawed view of goodness and self-righteousness. | He read the passage in context with the rich young ruler rather than as a denial of Jesus' deity. Jesus' question presses the man's careless use of "good" and exposes his assumption that he has kept the commandments, leading to the demand to give up possessions and follow Jesus; the point is rhetorical and diagnostic, not a Unitarian proof text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=5931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:51</a> | Is Leviticus 18:22 only about men with boys rather than condemning male same-sex relations generally? | He said the Hebrew does not refer to boys, is directed to men concerning males, and uses the strongest category of condemnation, though he answered cautiously because of platform rules. | He answered cautiously because of platform rules, but said the Hebrew wording does not support limiting the verse to men with boys. He treated the text as addressed to adult men concerning males more generally and said it uses a severe category of condemnation, so the proposed narrower reading does not fit the language as he understood it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=6323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:23</a> | Could `40` be idiomatic for a generation, making Moses' 120 years about three generations? | He said that is possible and better than rigid literalism in some cases, but `40` is more broadly an idiom for an uncertain long time and does not always mean a generation. | He allowed that forty can sometimes map onto a generation and that reading Moses' 120 years as three forty-year spans can be plausible. But he emphasized that forty is broader than "one generation"; in many texts it functions as an idiom for a long, indeterminate, or complete period, so each use still has to be read in context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=6607" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:07</a> | Does the biblical text clearly say Pharaoh and all his army drowned in the Reed Sea? | He said no. He argued that Psalm 136 speaks poetically of God shaking off Pharaoh and his army, while Exodus and archaeology do not require Pharaoh himself or Egypt's whole army to have drowned. | He said Exodus does not require Pharaoh personally to have drowned or Egypt's entire military to have been destroyed. He read Psalm 136's language about Pharaoh poetically, argued that the chariot corps in the eastern Delta is the likely force involved, and noted that known royal bodies do not show signs one would expect from drowning in that kind of disaster. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=7321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:01</a> | Did Yeshua lie at the wedding at Cana when he told Mary that his time had not yet come? | He said no. Jesus was signaling that the miracle was a one-off, not the main timing or purpose of his ministry, while still honoring his mother and saving the wedding party from shame. | He said Jesus was not lying, because "my hour has not yet come" refers to the broader timing and purpose of his public ministry, not an absolute refusal to act. By doing the miracle quietly, Jesus honored Mary and spared the wedding hosts shame while indicating that the sign was not the full arrival of his appointed hour. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=7787" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:47</a> | Is there a contradiction between Genesis 1:28's command to multiply and 1 Corinthians 7's claim that being unmarried can be better? | No. He said Genesis 1 concerns the creation mandate to fill the earth, while 1 Corinthians 7 concerns undistracted devotion to God; Paul still says marriage is not sin. | He distinguished a creation-level mandate from pastoral counsel in a particular Christian context. Genesis 1 commands humanity to be fruitful and fill the earth, while Paul in 1 Corinthians 7 says singleness can be advantageous for undistracted service; Paul also explicitly says marriage is not sin, so the two texts address different questions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=8341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:01</a> | What is fantasy, truth, or possible evidence for angelic infiltration in ancient aliens, ghosts, and related phenomena? | He dismissed ancient aliens as fantasy but treated ghost phenomena as complex, with some apparitions, hauntings, and poltergeist-like activity possibly involving spiritual beings. | He rejected ancient-aliens explanations as fantasy, but he did not dismiss all anomalous experiences. He said ghost reports need discernment because some may be ordinary, some may be apparitions or haunting-type phenomena, and some could involve spiritual beings; he kept the category broad rather than making every case demonic or fraudulent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=8803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:43</a> | Who were Yeshua's brothers, and how were they related to him? | He said the answer depends heavily on denominational tradition: Catholic cousins, Orthodox Joseph's children from an earlier marriage, or Protestant younger children of Joseph and Mary; he favored the Orthodox or Protestant options as stronger than the cousin view. | He laid out the main traditions: Roman Catholic interpretation often treats them as cousins, Orthodox tradition often as Joseph's children from an earlier marriage, and Protestant interpretation as later children of Joseph and Mary. He said the cousin view seemed weakest to him, while the Orthodox and Protestant options fit the data more naturally depending on one's tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=9105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:45</a> | Could a dream of a deceased Christian parent appearing healed be genuine? | He said it is possible that someone dead in Christ could appear for a special purpose, while warning that Scripture forbids seeking contact with the dead. | He said the scenario is possible, especially if the person is dead in Christ and the appearance is not being sought through forbidden practices. He drew a line between God allowing an appearance for some purpose and people trying to contact the dead, which Scripture forbids, so the experience should be received cautiously rather than pursued. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=9341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:41</a> | What are his biggest unanswered questions in biblical studies? | He highlighted early Hebrew paleography, identifying Abraham's Dynasty 13 pharaoh, clarifying the later Exodus route, the Ark's side decoration, and the flood's timing. | He listed several unresolved questions he would like answered: details of early Hebrew paleography, the identity of Abraham's Dynasty 13 pharaoh, the later Exodus route, the side decoration of the Ark, and the timing of the flood. These were presented as open research interests rather than settled conclusions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=9667" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:07</a> | Does Deuteronomy 23:15-16 mean the Mosaic law allows a slave to leave if they choose? | He said the passage appears to prohibit returning an escaped slave, but cautioned that Israelite slave law is specialized and the practical consequences could differ by the kind of slavery involved. | He said the text appears to forbid returning an escaped slave to the master and tells Israel not to oppress the fugitive. He cautioned, however, that Israelite slavery was not one simple institution; the implications would vary depending on whether the person was an Israelite debt servant, a foreign slave, or another legal category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=10344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:24</a> | What does he think of John Walton's book on demons? | He had not read it yet, though he was curious about it and noted he was behind on conference book purchases. | He said he had not yet read Walton's book on demons, so he could not evaluate its argument directly. He expressed curiosity about it and noted that he was behind on books he had picked up or intended to pick up around conferences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=10719" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:39</a> | Was Elisha like Thor after recovering the lost axe head from the Jordan River? | He said the point is not the axe head itself but water manipulation as a prophetic sign, comparable to Moses, Joshua, Elijah, Elisha, and Egyptian traditions about wise men. | He treated the joke as pointing toward a serious biblical pattern: the miracle is less about the axe head and more about prophetic mastery over water. He linked Elisha's act to a broader series of water-manipulation signs associated with Moses, Joshua, Elijah, Elisha, and Egyptian wise-man traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=11226" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:06</a> | Is John Walton right that demons in the Bible are not ontologically real but only literary devices? | He rejected that view, saying demons are real and similar to angels in ontology, with differences of alignment and corrupted function. | He rejected the claim that demons are merely literary devices. In his answer, demons are real beings comparable to angels in ontology, but corrupted or opposed in function and allegiance, so biblical demon language should not be reduced to a non-realist literary category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=11415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:15</a> | Is Jesus the ultimate water bender because he changed water into wine? | He treated the comment as having a real point: changing water into wine is a form of water manipulation that demonstrates Jesus' prophetic status as equal to Moses. | He accepted the playful framing only insofar as it highlights a real biblical sign pattern. Turning water into wine is water manipulation, and he connected it to signs that place Jesus in continuity with, and superior to, Moses-like prophetic authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=11499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:39</a> | Is it crazy that a deceased mother explained the Trinity in a dream after prayer and fasting? | He said not necessarily, but dreams require discernment because some may be ordinary subconscious activity, some genuine communication, and some deceptive spiritual manipulation. | He said the experience is not automatically crazy, especially in the context of prayer and fasting, but he urged discernment. Dreams can arise from the subconscious, from genuine divine or permitted communication, or from deceptive spiritual sources, so the content should be tested rather than accepted uncritically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=12303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:03</a> | From his study, is praying to the saints biblical? | He said Scripture does not clearly settle it and the question depends on church tradition; he was more comfortable praying directly to God and warned that not every alleged saint may truly be safe to invoke. | He said the biblical evidence does not clearly settle the practice and that one's answer will depend heavily on church tradition. He personally preferred praying directly to God, did not condemn everyone who prays to saints, and cautioned that one should not invoke questionable figures or people who may not truly be saints. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/b3ytxeDVHGg?t=12946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:46</a> | Did the ancient Egyptians ever find dinosaur fossils or other prehistoric beasts? | Generally no. He said they clearly encountered fossil shells and brachiopods at places like Giza, but not the kinds of large dinosaur remains people usually imagine. | He said ancient Egyptians would not generally have encountered dinosaur fossils because Egypt was underwater or a runoff plain during much of the relevant geological periods. They did encounter fossils in limestone, especially brachiopods at Giza and the Sphinx Temple area, and perhaps whale fossils exist in Egypt, but he said there is no evidence Egyptians found large dinosaur or prehistoric reptile remains. |
